data.title: Update: Market Chatter: Jury Determines Fortress Controls VLSI in Intel-VLSI Patent Infringement Case
data.description:
(Updates with Fortress and Intel responses in the fifth paragraph.)

Intel could possibly escape over $3 billion in patent-infringement verdicts after the US District Court for the Western District of Texas determined Thursday that VLSI Technology is controlled by Fortress Investment Group, Bloomberg reported.

The jury determined that VLSI and Finjan are both controlled by the asset management firm Fortress, the news report said.

During the three-day trial, the chipmaker argued that its 2012 licensing agreement with Finjan covered VLSI's patents as the two companies are affiliated, the report said.

The verdict could help Intel in furthering its efforts to push the court to ultimately overturn a November 2022 verdict of over $900 million and a similar verdict of $2.2 billion that VLSI won against Intel, the report said.

Fortress and Intel declined to comment when reached by MT Newswires. VLSI didn't immediately respond to a request for comment.

Disclaimer

Market Chatter news is derived from conversations with market professionals globally. This information is believed to be from reliable sources but may include rumor and speculation. Accuracy is not guaranteed.

data.title: Cooper Companies Down Over 13%, on Pace for Largest Percent Decrease Since March 2020 — Data Talk
data.description:
Cooper Companies, Inc. (COO) is currently at $69.39, down $10.57 or 13.22%

  • Would be lowest close since Nov. 9, 2022, when it closed at $68.39
  • On pace for largest percent decrease since March 12, 2020, when it fell 13.54%
  • Currently down eight of the past nine days
  • Currently down three consecutive days; down 13.9% over this period
  • Worst three-day stretch since the three days ending May 9, 2022, when it fell 14.03%
  • Down 15.04% month-to-date; on pace for worst month since March 2020, when it fell 15.07%
  • Down 24.52% year-to-date
  • Down 39.12% from its all-time closing high of $113.98 on Sept. 3, 2021
  • Down 26.42% from 52 weeks ago (May 31, 2024), when it closed at $94.31
  • Down 37.62% from its 52-week closing high of $111.23 on Sept. 16, 2024
  • Would be a new 52-week closing low
  • Traded as low as $65.95; lowest intraday level since Nov. 3, 2022, when it hit $64.32
  • Down 17.52% at today's intraday low; largest intraday percent decrease since Nov. 15, 2011, when it fell as much as 19.01%

All data as of 10:23:28 AM ET

Source: Dow Jones Market Data, FactSet

data.title: Embracing Mountains and Seas, Coexisting with Cultures: The "International Mountain Tourism Day 2025" Theme Events Officially Kick Off in Gran Canaria, Spain
data.description:
Gran Canaria, Spain--(Newsfile Corp. - May 30, 2025) - On May 29, 2025 local time, the "International Mountain Tourism Day 2025" Theme Events officially kicked off in Gran Canaria, Spain. Centered around the theme "Embracing Mountains and Seas, Coexisting with Cultures-Mountain Tourism and Cultural Inclusiveness for the Future," the event brought together a diverse group of prestigious international organizations, government officials, industry leaders, experts, and scholars, and media professionals from around the world. The gathering aims to explore paths of inclusive and reciprocal global mountain tourism and shared development.

Scene from the Opening Ceremony

A series of activities were held as part of the Theme Events, including the Opening Ceremony, the Theme Forum, the Exchange and Sharing Session of Mountain Culture and Tourism, the China-Spain Cultural and Tourism Exchange Exhibition, and the "Night of Gran Canaria" Welcome Dinner. Guests participated in various formats to exchange advanced ideas, discuss frontier issues, and deepen practical cooperation. At the Opening Ceremony, keynote addresses were delivered by Dominique de Villepin, Chairman of the International Mountain Tourism Alliance (IMTA); Miguel Sanz, General Director of Turespaña; Antonio Morales Méndez, President of the Government of Gran Canaria, Spain; Christine Brew, Representative of UN Tourism; and Yang Changqing, Director of the Oficina Nacional de Turismo de China en Madrid. Liang Nan, Counsellor of the Embassy of the People's Republic of China in Spain, was specially designated by H.E. Yao Jing, Ambassador Extraordinary and Plenipotentiary to the Kingdom of Spain and the Principality of Andorra, to attend the ceremony and deliver a speech on his behalf. He Yafei, Vice Chairman and Secretary-General of IMTA, and Song Xiaolu, Deputy Director General of Department of Culture and Tourism of Guizhou Province, China, also delivered insightful remarks.

At the event, Dominique de Villepin, Chairman of the International Mountain Tourism Alliance (IMTA), and Christine Brew, Representative of UN Tourism, exchanged a Memorandum of Understanding, signaling a future of constructive cooperation in promoting innovative governance in mountain tourism and advancing sustainable development. Peter Semone, Chairman of the Pacific Asia Travel Association (PATA), released the Asia-Pacific Mountain Tourism Development Report (2025), jointly compiled by IMTA and PATA. The report provides a systematic analysis of the far-reaching impact of sustainable tourism on economic growth, cultural exchange, and ecological conservation. It also offers forward-looking strategic insights and professional recommendations for the development of mountain tourism in the Asia-Pacific region. The event also witnessed the release of the Enjoy the Wonder of Mountains, Share the Natural Beauty-The "International Mountain Tourism Day 2025" Gran Canaria Initiative. The initiative, grounded in the concepts of mutual learning among civilizations and integrative innovation, calls on governments, enterprises, communities, and other stakeholders to strengthen cooperation, actively promote environmentally friendly tourism, and jointly build an open, inclusive, and mutually enriching mountain civilization.

Dominique de Villepin, Chairman of the International Mountain Tourism Alliance (IMTA), and Christine Brew, Representative of UN Tourism, exchanged a Memorandum of Understanding (MoU)

"The IMTD has now emerged as a vital platform, not only showcasing the rich and varied values of our mountain regions but also fostering deeper exchanges within our industry and strengthening international collaboration," said Dominique de Villepin, Chairman of the International Mountain Tourism Alliance (IMTA). He emphasized that this year's Theme Events-held against the backdrop of the 50th anniversary of diplomatic relations between China and the EU, and the 20th anniversary of the comprehensive strategic partnership between China and Spain-will carry forward the spirit of "Mutual Understanding among People." By bringing together the insights and expertise of industry leaders, it will be a powerful force in fostering cultural inclusiveness and deepening the bonds between China and Europe.

Miguel Sanz, General Director of Turespaña, noted that mountain tourism is one of the key directions for the future of global tourism. Turespaña is fully committed to promoting the sustainable transformation of Spain's tourism sector, aiming to achieve a balanced development that aligns environmental protection, social responsibility, and economic growth. The goal is to foster a new tourism model that is green, low-carbon, and enhances residents' well-being.

Liang Nan, Counsellor of the Embassy of the People's Republic of China in Spain, stated that on the occasion of the 20th anniversary of the establishment of the Comprehensive Strategic Partnership between China and Spain, he expressed hope that mountain tourism, as a unique vehicle, would further deepen cultural exchanges between the two countries. He also invited more Spanish friends to appreciate the grandeur of China's landscapes and the charm of its civilization, and to jointly open a new chapter in China-Spain tourism cooperation.

Christine Brew, Representative of UN Tourism, emphasized that mountain tourism is a vital tool for promoting inclusive development, environmental protection, and cultural heritage preservation-particularly through its strong linkages to community empowerment and rural revitalization. She underscored that mountain tourism plays a key role in advancing the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), directly contributing to multiple targets.

Scene from the Panel Discussion

The supporting activities were rich and vibrant. The China-Spain Cultural and Tourism Exchange Exhibition showcased the natural and cultural landscapes of mountain destinations in both countries through fine brush paintings and photographic works. The "Night of Gran Canaria" Welcome Dinner featured a spectacular blend of traditional Chinese intangible cultural heritage performances by ethnic minorities and the passionate expressions of Spanish folk art. Together, they created a vivid scene of mutual cultural appreciation and harmonious coexistence between civilizations, set against the backdrop of mountains and seas.

The event was co-hosted by the International Mountain Tourism Alliance (IMTA) and Turismo de Gran Canaria, with strong support from UN Tourism, the Pacific Asia Travel Association (PATA), the Global Tourism Economy Forum (GTEF), the Mountain Partnership Secretariat (MPS) hosted by the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO), the Oficina Nacional de Turismo de China en Madrid, the Guizhou Provincial Department of Culture and Tourism, China, and the Foreign Affairs Office of Guizhou Province (Guizhou Provincial People's Association for Friendship with Foreign Countries), China.

The "International Mountain Tourism Day" is an international commemorative day initiated by the International Mountain Tourism Alliance in 2018. Since its first launch on May 29, 2019, the theme events have been successfully held in China, Nepal, Laos, France, and Spain for seven consecutive years.

data.title: Baltic Dry Index Extends Gains, Books Monthly Gain
data.description:
The Baltic Exchange's dry bulk sea freight index, which tracks rates for ships carrying dry commodities, was up for the third day on Friday, climbing about 4.8% to a four-week high of 1,418 points, boosted by the larger vessel segment.

The capesize index, which typically transports 150,000-ton cargoes such as iron ore and coal, also extended gains for the third session, jumping 10.6% to its highest since April 3 at 2,277 points.

Meanwhile, the panamax index, which usually carries 60,000-70,000 tons of coal or grain, was down for a seventh straight session, falling about 1.6% to an over two-month low of 1,1319 points; and the supramax index eased five points to a near four-week low of 951 points.

The benchmark index posted a 5.8% weekly gain and a 2.3% weekly rise.

data.title: MULTI-PLATINUM ARTIST DYLAN SCOTT ENTERS EASY DOES IT PHASE OF LIFE WITH THIRD STUDIO ALBUM, OUT NOW
data.description:
Nashville, Tenn., May 30, 2025 (GLOBE NEWSWIRE) — Multi-Platinum recording artist Dylan Scott enters a new creative chapter with total confidence as his third studio album Easy Does It releases today. The 11-track collection spans themes of heartbreak, committed love and nods to the small town that raised him. The Tennessean praised the album, stating, “Whether backed by a band or accompanied by musicians playing as soft as a whisper, Scott's gruff baritone, one of his most prominent artistic selling points, takes center stage.”

“I feel like I’m in the best stage of life so far, and this album reflects that for me,” Scott describes. “I don’t know if that’s because of my kids or being married as long as I have, or if it’s just getting into my 30s, but I don’t stress. I don’t worry like I used to – we just go to work. As crazy as it sounds, I’m in that Easy Does It phase of life.”

Easy Does It opens with Scott’s current Country radio single, “What He’ll Never Have,” which is already shaping up to be the biggest song of his career with over 300M global streams so far. Written by Scott alongside his brother Logan, tour manager and guitar player Robbie Gatlin and songwriter Ricky Rowton, the "infectious, cleverly composed track" (Holler) continues to rise on the Country Airplay charts. The album also features Scott’s most recent No. 1, “This Town’s Been Too Good To Us,” lively “I Owe You One,” which Scott performed on NBC’s TODAY and energetic anthems like “Country Till I Die” and “I Hate Whiskey,” the latter of which MusicRow described as “a clever twist on a honky-tonk trope, delivered by a solid country hit maker.”

“There’s confidence that comes with success, no doubt about it, and I’m grateful for this Easy Does It stage,” he says. “I’m not the 20-year-old kid anymore, I’m 34 with kids and a wife, and I hope fans can see where I’m at in life. I hope they listen and go, ‘Hey, that’s different from the last album,’ and I hope people take something from it to help ‘em personally. But really, I just hope they see that I’m having fun and am not too worried about things. Just enjoying life.”

No stranger to love songs, the Louisiana native continues to deliver serenade-worthy tracks on Easy Does It. The picturesque “Back Forty,” written by Adam Craig, Kyle Clark and Patrick Murphy, can be taken as a note right out of Scott’s Easy Does It handbook. A serene tribute to simple life, he stated he can’t sing the reflective ballad without thinking of the farmhouse outside Nashville where his kids are growing up, featuring a real-life acreage just off its back porch. The swaying acoustic snap track “Twice,” written by Charlie Handsome, Jaxson Free, John Byron and Taylor Phillips, captures the same romantic spirit as his first career No. 1 “My Girl,” just further down the road of life.

“I’ve been with my wife since I was 15 years old – she’s the rock, she’s the glue that holds this whole thing together,” Scott explains. “I didn’t write ‘Twice,’ but I knew I had to have it the first time I heard it, because it just reminds me of my wife and our relationship. If I could, I wish I could live it twice.”

Scott’s 2025 has already seen several high notes, including snagging two No. 1s so far this year with “This Town’s Been Too Good To Us” and “Boys Back Home” with Dylan Marlowe, adding to his six career No. 1 singles overall. He continues to dominate on the road, recently wrapping his headlining Country Till I Die Tour. The tour saw 10 sold-out stops as well as Scott’s headlining arena debut with two arena shows. The dates became his highest-grossing and highest-selling tour dates yet, shattering his previous attendance records.

The road warrior will head back on the road this fall on his headlining Easy Does It Tour, set to hit 15 cities throughout September and October. Special guests George Birge, Josh Ross and Zach John King will join him on select dates. Next week, fans can catch Scott at CMA Fest in Nashville, where he will perform at Spotify House on June 6 and Nissan Stadium on June 8.

Keep up with Scott on Instagram, TikTok and Facebook. For tickets and more information, please visit dylanscottcountry.com.

data.title: NetApp's Fiscal Q4 Supported by 'Strong' AFA, Public Cloud Growth but Outlook Misses Consensus, Oppenheimer Says
data.description:
NetApp's fiscal Q4 performance was supported by "strong" All-flash array and public cloud growth, but the company again provided outlook below consensus, Oppenheimer said in a Friday note.

The lower-than-expected outlook at midpoint was due to broader macro challenges, US tariffs, public sector weakness, and spending delays in European regions, analyst Param Singh wrote.

Although NetApp reported 150 artificial intelligence wins in fiscal Q4, product revenue growth will still require "multiple" quarters to be back to a mid-single-digit growth path, the analyst added.

"NetApp's GM was pressured by a heavier product mix and rising component costs, with tariff and NAND pricing expected to persist as margin headwinds in FY26," Singh said.

Oppenheimer maintained its perform rating on the stock.

Shares of the company were up almost 1% in recent Friday trading.

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1026 ET - Despite the recession talk fueled by U.S. trade policy, Canada's economy has held up rather well so far in 2025, writes Doug Porter, chief economist at BMO Capital Markets. The GDP data for 1Q, and the early advance for April output, "are sending no obvious distress signals," Porter says. He acknowledges the 2.2% annualized gain in 1Q masks weakness in the household sector, as exports and inventory accumulation did the heavy lifting. Further, Statistics Canada projects a 0.1% GDP gain in April from March, which he touts as "amazingly resilient" given the month was dominated by trade and political uncertainty here. BMO now believes the Bank of Canada leaves its main interest rate unchanged next week, dropping its previous call for a cut. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1014 ET - There wasn't much to celebrate in Canada's 1Q GDP report, says Andrew Hencic, economist at TD Bank. The headline measure indicates a solid 2.2% annualized gain in the quarter, which Hencic says is attributed to the potential front running of tariffs leading up to a buildup in business inventories, and purchases of machinery and equipment. The report suggests consumers have taken the proverbial foot off the gas, with final domestic demand declining in the January-to-March period. "Trade tensions and the uncertainty they heaped on the economy have started to show through on activity," Hencic says, adding there are "real cracks emerging in the economy." He predicts two more rate cuts from the Bank of Canada in 2025 to give the economy more breathing room. (paul.vieira@wsj.com, @paulvieira)

1006 ET - U.S. tariffs are more negative for the U.S. economy and for the dollar than for other countries and currencies, Bank of America analysts write. Tariffs create the risk of retaliation and the U.S. "trades more with the rest of the world than the rest of the world trades" with the U.S., they say. Data will determine the fate of the dollar. If U.S. indicators are positive, "we would expect investors to start ignoring the policy noise" and support the greenback. But BofA is bracing for bearish data, as policy uncertainty paralyzes hiring and investment plans and tariffs get higher. Fiscal policy loosening should result in higher borrowing costs, they say. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0951 ET - Don't let the solid headline on Canada's 1Q GDP report fool you, says Royce Mendes, economist at Desjardins Group. The domestic economy looks "very frail," and should provide impetus to Bank of Canada officials to cut its benchmark interest rate by a quarter point next week despite accelerating core inflation. Output climbed 2.2% annualized in 1Q, powered by businesses looking to get ahead of tariffs. Yet, final domestic demand contracted 0.1% in 1Q. Given the volatility in the quarter due to U.S. trade-policy factors, "the reading on final domestic demand provides a clear signal on the health of the economy," Mendes tells clients. He says the GDP data signal the economy was already stalling before the full impact of tariffs kicked in. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0947 ET - Another weak point in Canada's 1Q GDP report — which surprised with a 2.2% annualized gain — is terms of trade, or the difference between the value of exports versus imports. Statistics Canada says that in 1Q, import prices rose more than export prices, leading to a drop in the terms of trade of 0.6% nonannualized, hitting its lowest level since mid-2023. Canada has benefited from increases in terms of trade through higher commodity prices, and thereby lifting national income. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0943 ET - U.S. President Trump's tightening of immigration controls could present a headwind to digital assets, Zaye Capital Markets analyst Naeem Aslam says in a note. "For tech investors and digital asset markets--where the free movement of global talent is core--such moves might presage constraints in the future, impacting innovation cycles and digital asset flows." That comes amid Trump's expanding foray into cryptocurrencies, including the establishment of a strategic bitcoin reserve. Trump's efforts have improved bitcoin's mainstream adoption but also bring ethical and regulatory issues, he says. Bitcoin last trades down 0.6% at $105,578 after hitting a 10-day low of $104,648 earlier, according to LSEG. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0940 ET - A recession in the U.S. or eurozone due to tariffs could cause credit spreads to widen materially to levels seen after the collapse of Credit Suisse, Societe Generale credit strategist Juan Valencia says in a note. The risk of a recession in the two regions, nonetheless, remains low but it is worth monitoring, Valencia says. The damage from tariffs is likely to cause economic slow down but "it is not enough to push the U.S. or European economies into recession," he says. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0931 ET - The Fed is likely to see the benign PCE inflation report "as the calm before the storm," Fitch Rating's Olu Sonola says in a note. "They will continue to wait for the storm-unless consumer spending buckles and the unemployment rate rises rapidly," he writes. In the April report, personal consumption increased 0.8%. Disposable personal income and consumption also increased. Annual inflation ticked lower, to just a hair above the Fed's 2% target. "This report shows that the U.S. consumer remains resilient," Sonola says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

0927 ET - The Canadian dollar rises after data showed Canada's economy grew by more than expected in the first quarter. Gross domestic product rose by an annualized 2.2% rate, compared to 2.1% in the previous quarter, Statistics Canada reported Friday. That exceeds the 1.6% growth expected by analysts in a WSJ survey. Growth was driven by exports as U.S. companies stockpiled goods in anticipation of tariffs. The U.S. dollar falls to an intra-day low of 1.3788 Canadian dollars from 1.3808 beforehand. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0926 ET - Canada's better-than-anticipated 2.2% annualized 1Q was "flattered" by tariff front running, says CIBC Captial Markets economist Andrew Grantham. Exports surged 6.7% and businesses added to their inventories. Grantham notes domestic demand was weak, reflecting diminished confidence among households and decisions by businesses to either scrap or scale back investment plans amid elevated trade-policy uncertainty. An early estimate for growth in April pointed to a 0.1% month-over-month advance, fueled by the commodity sector. Grantham says early tracking for 2Q, assuming flat readings for May and June, indicates meager growth of 0.5%, or well below the economy's long-run potential, "suggesting that slack is building up again." (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

0920 ET - Canadian GDP growth in 1Q may have surprised to the upside at 2.2% annualized, but a contraction in domestic demand has Capital Economics sticking with its view the Bank of Canada will again cut interest rates next week. The pace was stronger than the flash estimate for a 1.5% rise, though economist Stephen Brown says that likely in part reflects the downward revision to 4Q 2024 growth to 2.1% from 2.6%. Either way, he says the details are concerning. Most of the growth came from net trade, with a 6.7% annualized rise in exports amid tariff front-running in the U.S. Canadian consumption growth slowed to 1.2% annualized, gross fixed capital formation sank 3.1% and government spending surprisingly contracted a modest 0.3%. Capital expects 2Q GDP growth will slow toward 0.5% at best. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

0901 ET - Dollar indexes are flat relative to yesterday's close as trade tensions gain momentum while a key U.S. inflation gauge inches closer to the Fed's target. Trump lashes out against China's trade practices, in a social media post. Meanwhile, April PCE inflation cools to 2.1% from 2.3%. The Fed targets 2%. However, markets keep pricing a hold on interest rates through September and a total of two cuts this year. Next week's labor report is likely to be scrutinized for signs of policy impact on employment. The dollar weakens 0.3% against the yen, while strengthening 0.2% to the euro.(paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

data.title: Agrana Set to Take Full Ownership of Austria Juice GmbH
data.description:
Agrana (AGR.VI) agreed to purchase RWA Raiffeisen Ware Austria's 49.99% stake in their Austria Juice GmbH joint venture for 54.7 million euros.

The acquisition is part of Agrana's plans to bolster its newly established food and beverage solutions division, according to a Friday filing.

Austria Juice, which generates 330 million euros in annual revenue, has 13 production sites in Austria, Germany, Hungary, Poland, Romania, Ukraine and China. It will be fully owned by the Austrian agricultural raw materials company when the deal closes.

data.title: American Consumers Are Still Gloomy on the Economy — WSJ
data.description:
By Chao Deng

American households still felt gloomy about the economy in May, a month when changing tariff policies made it difficult to forecast prices.

The final May reading for the University of Michigan's closely watched index of consumer sentiment was 52.2, unchanged from the previous month.

It was slightly higher than a preliminary reading of 50.8 that was released two weeks ago. It was still one of the lowest ever recorded, in data going back to 1952.

Write to Chao Deng at chao.deng@wsj.com

data.title: Brazil tightens prudential rules, adds individual liquidity requirements for banks
data.description:
Brazil's National Monetary Council (CMN) tightened prudential rules on risk management, liquidity and capital for financial institutions, introducing individual requirements to complement existing rules for conglomerates.

According to a central bank statement on Friday, the changes approved by the country's top economic policy body strengthen the stability of the financial system and align with the international Basel III regulatory framework as well as recommendations from the IMF and World Bank.

The government will introduce a liquidity requirement taking effect in July 2026 for standalone financial institutions, aligning with the methodology already used for financial conglomerates.

The rule will apply to institutions that are part of conglomerates classified under "Segment 1," which includes large and systemically important entities subject to stricter regulatory standards.

Further changes to integrated risk management, effective in September, will seek to ensure timely liquidity transfers among institutions within the same conglomerate, the central bank said.

Meanwhile, the leverage ratio calculation has been updated and expanded to cover all institutions except those with a low-risk profile. Its implementation will be phased in between July 2026 and January 2028.

According to the central bank, the new requirement will apply on both consolidated and individual bases, including to payment institutions that lead large conglomerates integrated by financial institutions.
